coredata.dc:description: Exploration of robust antiseptics with antibiofilm performance and long-term antimicrobial activity is of great significance to effectively destroy foodborne pathogenic bacteria. Herein, a silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) /carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposite (Ag@CDs) was constructed by the reaction of Ag+ with CDs, which has strong antibacterial ability, and mighty biofilm inhibition and destruction ability toward E. coli and S. aureus. Notably, low concentration of Ag@CDs (40.0 and 80.0 μg/mL) could ablate 86.11 % E. coli and 75.86 % S. aureus biomass of mature biofilm. To our knowledge, this is the first bactericide with powerful biofilm destruction activity. In addition, Ag@CDs exhibited long-term (30 d) antibacterial effect on the substrates of plastic, cotton, gauze, and glass. Ag@CDs was evenly dispersed into the matrix of chitosan (CS)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to fabricate well-blended food packaging films. Among which, the 3 % Ag-CP film exhibited improved mechanical performance, excellent UV–Vis light blocking ability, and intensive antimicrobial activity. Despite seven days of storage, 3 % Ag-CP still could preserve the freshness and safety of the pork and shrimp, certifying it extends the storage life of packaged foods.

head.ce:title: Silver nanoparticles/carbon dots nanocomposite with potent antibiofilm and long-term antimicrobial activity doped into chitosan/polyvinyl alcohol film for food preservation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploration of robust antiseptics with antibiofilm performance and long-term antimicrobial activity is of great significance to effectively destroy foodborne pathogenic bacteria. Herein, a silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) /carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposite (Ag@CDs) was constructed by the reaction of Ag+ with CDs, which has strong antibacterial ability, and mighty biofilm inhibition and destruction ability toward E. coli and S. aureus. Notably, low concentration of Ag@CDs (40.0 and 80.0 μg/mL) could ablate 86.11 % E. coli and 75.86 % S. aureus biomass of mature biofilm. To our knowledge, this is the first bactericide with powerful biofilm destruction activity. In addition, Ag@CDs exhibited long-term (30 d) antibacterial effect on the substrates of plastic, cotton, gauze, and glass. Ag@CDs was evenly dispersed into the matrix of chitosan (CS)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to fabricate well-blended food packaging films. Among which, the 3 % Ag-CP film exhibited improved mechanical performance, excellent UV–Vis light blocking ability, and intensive antimicrobial activity. Despite seven days of storage, 3 % Ag-CP still could preserve the freshness and safety of the pork and shrimp, certifying it extends the storage life of packaged foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Since 2014, the number of hungry people worldwide has increased [1]. Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) 2 and SDG 3 are central to the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, which aims to achieve Zero Hunger and promote Good Health and Well-being. One of the key targets is to reduce food waste by 50 % by 2030 through minimizing food losses along production and value chains. This has intensified the focus on food transport, preservation, and quality [2,3]. On account of the high fat and protein characteristics of meat, lipid oxidation is extremely easy to occur during production, transportation and storage, accompanied by microbial growth [4,5]. Some pathogenic E. coli such as diarrheic Enteropathogenic E. coli (InPEC) and extrenteropathogenic E.coli (ExPEC), as well as S. aureus, can cause intestinal diseases and inflammatory infections [6,7]. Thus, suitable packaging materials need to be designed urgently to extend the shelf life of food, and reducing food waste is an important part of developing a sustainable economy, which will contribute to achieving SDG 2 and SDG 3 [8,9]. Although plastic packaging is commonly used for food preservation, it cannot effectively delay the oxidation and decay rate of food [10]. Furthermore, plastic is difficult to decompose in natural environment, which resulting in serious environmental pollution and health problems. In consideration of food's safety, quality, operating costs and environmental protection, developing low-cost and eco-friendly food packaging materials with degradability and antibacterial activities has become a top priority [11].

Chitosan (CS) is a kind of polymer polysaccharide derived from exoskeleton of crustaceans and shellfish. The superior characteristics of high biocompatibility, renewable, excellent film formation ability, and biodegradability render it a perfect substitute to artificial synthetic plastic for food preservation [12]. Regrettably, CS has the shortcomings of low thermal stability and mechanical strength. To address these issues, the combination of CS with other polymers is a wise choice [13].

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is widely used in food packaging, paint production, emulsifiers and dispersants [14,15]. PVA has the merits of low price, biodegradability, non-toxic and excellent mechanical properties. In bacterial contaminated environments, the end products of PVA decomposes are water and CO2. Thus, its widespread use does not pollute the environment [16]. PVA can blend with CS to form PVA/CS composite films with improved mechanical performance and stronger hydrophobicity [12]. Moreover, to ensure long-term preservation of food efficiently, incorporating bactericides are highly desired.

Silver-based nanoformulations are one of the most widely used antibacterial agents [17], which have excellent antimicrobial activity against >650 species of bacteria, with low dosage and high efficiency [18,19]. Silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) destroy the bacterial cytoplasm and cell membrane by releasing silver ions [20], consequently avoiding the emergence of super-resistant bacteria. Based on this, Ag NPs have found extensive applications in textiles, food, and pharmaceuticals. However, their widespread use has resulted in significant environmental release, primarily into aquatic ecosystems [21]. Excess Ag NPs can potentially harm bacteria, algae, fungi, invertebrates, plants, and fish. Furthermore, through bioaccumulation and biomagnification, these NPs can adversely affect both the environment and human health [22]. Consequently, there is a need to develop antibacterial agents with reduced silver content and lower release rates to facilitate their safe use in food packaging.

Carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of simple synthesis and stable aqueous dispersion [23,24], which have been extensively studied in the areas such as bioimaging [25,26], information encryption [27,28], nanovaccines [29–33], nanosensors [34,35], and nanofungicides [36,37]. A large number of studies have proved that CDs have good biocompatibility. CDs-C12 prepared by Yang et al. had high bactericidal ability, but in vivo toxicity test showed that CDs-C12 had no damage to major organs of mice [38]. In some cases, CDs can even protect cells from the damage caused by oxidative stress [39]. The Ce-doped CDs prepared by Zhang et al. can resist excessive inflammation and accelerate wound healing [40]. In addition, CDs can also be used as antibacterial agents and antioxidants to extend the shelf life of food. The CDs/PVA film prepared by Zhao et al. has been successfully used for the preservation of bananas, dates and fried meatballs [41].

Although some researchers have reported that Ag NPs-based film can be used for food preservation, the preparation process of Ag NPs is complicated and the long-term antibacterial activity is unstable. At the same time, research on the preservation capabilities of Ag NPs/CDs nanocomposites remains limited, especially in the context of meat preservation. This study aims to develop an Ag NPs/CDs nanocomposite with a simple preparation method and long-term antibacterial activity, and to evaluate the effectiveness of Ag NPs/CDs-based film in extending the shelf life of meat under both refrigerated and room temperature conditions. Herein, CDs were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and L-histidine as precursors. Ag@CDs NPs were successfully fabricated by simply stirring the mixture of CDs and AgNO3 solution without any additional reductant, stabilizer and toxic solvents. In addition, the doping content of Ag in Ag@CDs is as low as 1.25 % (w/t), which can effectively avoid large release of Ag into the environment and food. Ag@CDs has strong antibacterial activity, which can inhibit 97.21 % of E. coli biomass and 98.30 % of S. aureus biomass. More importantly, just low concentration of Ag@CDs can effectively disrupt 86.11 % E. coli and 75.86 % S. aureus biomass of mature biofilm, which is unique for the existing antibacterial agents. In addition, Ag@CDs exhibits long-term (30 d) inhibitory activity toward E. coli and S. aureus on various substrates (plastic, cotton, gauze, and glass). Benefiting from its notable biofilm inhibition and ablation activity, along with long-lasting antibacterial activity, Ag@CDs was dropped into the CS/PVA polymer blend to prepare active packaging film Ag-CS/PVA (named as Ag-CP). The 3 % Ag-CP film had enhanced tensile strength and potent light barrier property, as well as low moisture content (MC), water solubility (WS) and water vapor permeability (WVP). Furthermore, 3 % Ag-CP film had strong antimicrobial activity, which was employed as active food packaging for meat preservation. During packaged with 3 % Ag-CP film at 4 °C for 7 d, the color variation, total viable counts (TVC), weight loss, pH, and total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) of pork and shrimp were monitored. In addition, the 3 % Ag-CP film-packed pork was successfully stored for 4 d at room temperature. The results demonstrated 3 % Ag-CP had durable freshness maintaining performance, and could serve as an ecofriendly packaging candidates for food preservation. (Fig. 1 ).

CDs was prepared by the pyrolysis of PEI and histidine. PEI (1.2 g) and histidine (0.6 g) were dissolved in deionized water and pyrolyzed at 170 °C until the solution turned brown. The crude product was collected and centrifuged to remove the sediment. The solution was dialyzed (Molecular weight cutoff: 3500 Da) for 8 h, and the dialysate was lyophilized to obtain CDs. The aqueous solution of AgNO3 (75 mg) and CDs (15 mg) was stirred for overnight under dark condition, the over-sized products were then removed by the centrifugation (3500 rpm, 5 min). The supernatant was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and followed by three consecutive washes with water to obtain Ag@CDs.

Four different substrates including glass, cotton, gauze and plastic were prepared and dipped into Ag@CDs solution (40 or 80 μg/mL), dried at 45 °C and stored at room temperature. Substrates were taken out and cultured with S. aureus or E. coli (1 × 105 CFU/mL) for 4 h on the day 1 (after 1 day of storage), 10, 15 and 30. Then, substrates in bacterial solution were taken out, placed on agar plate and incubated for 24 h. At the same time, resazurin (0.2 mg/mL, 0.5 mL) was added to the bacterial solution with substrates treated, and the color change was observed after incubation at 37 °C for 4 h. The substrates without Ag@CDs solution treatment were used as the control groups.

The films were obtained by solution casting method. CS was dissolved in 1 % v/v acetic acid aqueous solution and stirred at 70 °C for 3 h to obtain 2 wt% CS solution. PVA was dissolved in deionized water and stirred at 80 °C for 3 h to obtain 3 wt% PVA solution [42]. The CS and PVA were mixed in equal mass, and 25 % glycerin (w/w, relative to the weight of CS and PVA) was added as a plasticizer. The mixture was stirred for 2 h and then sonicated to obtain the film forming solution. The film forming solution (8 mL) was carefully added to the petri dish (diameter = 9 cm) and treated at 45 °C for 8 h to obtain the CS-PVA films (2CS-PVA, CS-PVA and CS-2PVA, respectively). On the basis of the weight of CS and PVA, 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, 4 %, 5 % Ag@CDs were added into the composite solution, stirred away from light, transferred to a petri dish, and treated at 45 °C for 8 h to obtain Ag-CP films. According to the content of Ag@CDs, they were named as 1 % Ag-CP, 2 % Ag-CP, 3 % Ag-CP, 4 % Ag-CP and 5 % Ag-CP film, respectively.

The bacteriostatic property of Ag-CP films toward E. coli and S. aureus was detected by inhibition circle method. Each film (CS-PVA, 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, 4 % and 5 % Ag-CP films) was prepared as a 6 mm circular sheet and then transferred to a petri dish coated with bacteria solution (1.0 × 107 CFU/mL, 100 μL) and incubated for 24 h. The antibacterial zone size of different films was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial analysis of pork and shrimp
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh pork and freshwater shrimp were purchased from a local supermarket. Pork was cut into small pieces and the shells of shrimps were removed. The pork pieces and shrimp were divided into three groups: control (no treatment), CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP group. In the treatment groups, the pork and shrimp were wrapped with CS-PVA or 3 % Ag-CP film. The first day of the preservation experiment was recorded as Day 0, TVB-N content and pH of pork and shrimp were recorded on Day 0. The beginning of the preservation experiment was recorded as day 0. All the samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 7 d, photographed and weighed daily. On Day 7, the sample was mashed and filtered. pH, TVB-N content (Kjeldahl method) and TVC were measured. In addition, the pork preservation experiment was conducted at room temperature, pH, TVB-N content and TVC were measured on Day 4.

All experiments were repeated at least 3 times and presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was calculated using unpaired Student's two-sided t-test, and statistically significant differences are shown with *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of CDs and Ag@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image (Fig. 2a) depicts that CDs are monodisperse with the average diameter of 13.18 ± 2.55 nm. (Fig. 2b). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs exhibits a prominent peak at a 2θ value of 21o, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon [43–46], as depicted in Fig. S1. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2c) have two peaks at 230 nm and 348 nm. Generally, the maximum absorption peak at 230 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition from aromatic carbon [17]. The absorption peak at 348 nm is attributed to the n−π* transitions of the C=O/C=N bonds [44,47]. CDs exhibit a typical excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior, the strongest fluorescence was obtained under 340 nm excitation. The XPS full spectrum showed three typical elements C, N and O in CDs (Fig. 2d). The high-resolution (HR) XPS spectrum of C1s have four characteristic peaks at 284.5, 284.8, 285.5, and 287.3 eV, which are attributed to CC, CC, C-O/C-N, and C=N/C=O, respectively (Fig. S2a). The peaks at 398.3, 399.2, and 400.3 eV in the HR N1s spectrum correspond to pyridine N, amino N, and pyrolysis N, respectively (Fig. S2b). The peaks at 530.6, 531.7 and 532.2 eV in the HR O1s spectrum assigned to CO, CO and OH (Fig. S2c) [36]. The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized by FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2e). CDs have an obvious characteristic peak at 3390 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of N-H/O-H, indicating that CDs contain abundant amino and hydroxyl groups. It is shown that Ag+ was reduced to Ag NPs on the surface of CDs using CDs with excellent electron-donating ability as the reducing agent. Stretching and bending vibrations of CH are observed at 2950 and 1460 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 1633, 1563, 1310 and 1045 cm−1 correspond to CO, CC, CN and CO vibrations, respectively. The zeta potential of CDs was + 18.7 mV (Fig. 2f).

After the mixture of AgNO3 to CDs was stirred for overnight, the solution turned brown, signifying that Ag@CDs were formed. The morphology and size of Ag@CDs NPs were determined by TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS). Fig. 3a shows that Ag@CDs have good polydispersity and uniform size. The average size is 51.44 ± 7.29 nm (Fig. 3b). The hydrodynamic diameter (HD) of Ag@CDs determined by DLS is 62.2 nm (Fig. 3c). Compared with CDs, Ag@CDs shows a new absorption peak at 435 nm (Fig. 3d), which is associated with the surface plasmon resonance band (PRB) of Ag NPs [48], The symmetric shape of the PRB indicates that the formed silver NPs are spherical and monodisperse [18]. The fluorescence of CDs is completely quenched, indicated the successful preparation of Ag@CDs. The full scan XPS spectrum of Ag@CDs shows the existence of C, N, O and Ag elements (Fig. 3e). Four peaks (284.5, 284.8, 285.2 and 287.13 eV) in the high-resolution (HR) XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. S3a) are attributed to CC, CC, C-O/C-N and CN groups. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S3b) can be deconvoluted into the peaks of pyridine N (398.3 eV), amino N (399.2 eV) and pyrolysis N (400.3 eV). The O1s spectrum (Fig. S3c) can be assigned to CO (530.6 eV), CO (531.7 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (532.2 eV). The HR XPS spectrum of Ag 3d (Fig. 3f) has two main peaks of Ag 3d 3/2 (373.2 eV) and Ag 3d 5/2 (367.2 eV). The splitting of the Ag 3d doublet is 6.0 eV, indicating the presence of metallic silver [49]. The peaks at 373.6 and 367.6 eV are corresponding to Ag…O, whose splitting is also 6.0 eV, proves that Ag exists in the form of Ag0 [50]. The content of Ag NPs measured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer is about 1.25 % (w/t). The FTIR spectrum of Ag@CDs is depicted in Fig. 2e, compared with CDs, the signal at 3390 cm−1 is significantly weakened, which may be caused by the consumption of -NH2/-OH during the Ag+ reduction process. Ag+ was reduced to Ag NPs on the surface of CDs, due to the excellent electron-donating ability of CDs. At the same time, due to the interaction between CDs and Ag+, the peaks in the range of 1300–1650 cm−1 are also different [50]. The zeta potential of Ag@CDs was + 17.8 mV (Fig. 2f). These findings verify the successful fabrication of Ag@CDs.

The antibacterial effect of Ag@CDs was investigated using E. coli and S. aureus as experimental strains. The results showed that the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of Ag@CDs against E. coli and S. aureus was 40.0 and 80.0 μg/mL(Fig. 4a,b). The antibacterial capability of Ag@CDs was further verified by plate counting method (Fig. 4c,d and e), the minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) against E. coli and S. aureus was also 40.0 and 80.0 μg/mL.

Generally, bacterial infection propagated by biofilm formation is basically ineffective to the host immune response system, thus leading to chronic diseases. Bacterial biofilm is an extracellular matrix secreted by bacteria that wraps around the cell. The presence of bacterial biofilms increases bacterial resistance, which is detrimental to bacterial elimination [51]. The biofilm inhibition and biofilm destruction performance of Ag@CDs was evaluated by crystal violet (CV) staining assay. In the biofilm inhibition assay (Fig. 5a, b), the Ag@CDs treatment groups had no purple residue and the biomass accounted for <3 % after treated with Ag@CDs for 24 h, indicating that Ag@CDs could inhibit bacterial biofilms. Due to the protective effect of biofilms, it is difficult for traditional antibiotics to penetrate this barrier and achieve the clearance of biofilms [52,53]. Therefore, the ability of destructing biofilms is particularly important for antimicrobials. The CV staining results showed that Ag@CDs could destroy most of the formed biofilms in a short time (4 h). In the Ag@CDs treatment groups, the biofilm content of E. coli and S. aureus was 13.9 % and 24.1 %, indicating most of the biofilms had been destroyed by Ag@CDs (Fig. 5c,d). The above results suggest that Ag@CDs can not only prevent the biofilm formation, but also effectively disrupt mature biofilm. For all we know, this is the first report on nanoantibacterial agents possess inhibition and destruction biofilm capability.

Subsequently, the antibacterial effect of Ag@CDs was directly observed by live (SYTO 9, green fluorescence) and dead bacteria (propidium iodide (PI), red fluorescence) staining. Due to the disruption of bacterial cell membranes, PI can selectively stain dead bacteria, while SYTO 9 can label living cells by binding to nucleic acids [54]. The PBS groups of both bacteria showed green fluorescence (Fig. 6a), indicating the bacterial membranes were intact. After treatment with Ag@CDs, both E. coli and S. aureus showed strong red fluorescence, suggesting the bacteria were killed. Subsequently, we directly observed the morphologies of bacteria treated with Ag@CDs by scanning electron microscope (SEM) to evaluate the potential antibacterial mechanism of Ag@CDs (Fig. 6b,c). The appearance of bacteria in the Ag@CDs groups exhibited obvious collapse, some bacterial membranes were ruptured and the contents were leaked, suggesting that Ag@CDs can kill bacteria by destroying their cell membranes. By contrast, the bacteria in PBS groups were structurally intact. The positively charged Ag@CDs can induce electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged bacterial membranes, leading to damage of the cell membrane [36]. Concurrently, the silver atoms present on the Ag@CDs exhibit a strong affinity for sulfur- and phosphorus-containing biomolecules, such as DNA and a range of proteins within bacterial cells. This interaction contributes to the bactericidal effects of Ag@CDs, ultimately leading to bacterial cell death [45].

Ag@CDs solution was sprayed on different substrates (plastic, cotton, gauze, and glass). After dried at 45 °C, the coated substrates were stored tightly at room temperature. On day 1, 3, 5, and 30, the substrates were cultured with bacteria suspensions for 4 h, and then taken out. The treated bacteria suspensions were analyzed by resazurin assay, while the substrates were subjected to agar plating culturing for 24 h to evaluate the long-term antimicrobial performance of Ag@CDs (Fig. 7 ). When resazurin is reduced by enzymes in living cells, it changes from purple to pink [55,56]. In the control groups, the color of resazurin solution was pink, colonies were clearly observed around the substrates, confirming the growth and viability of bacterial cells. However, in the Ag@CDs groups, even after 30 d of storage at room temperature, the resazurin solution remained purple, and no obvious bacterial colonies were observed, except for the glass of S. aureus group, where a very small amount of colonies appeared. These findings definitely demonstrate that Ag@CDs have long-term antibacterial performance on various substrates.

Bacterial contamination is an important cause of meat spoilage, as well as a potential hazard to consumer dietary safety. The antibacterial effect of Ag-CP films was detected by agar diffusion method. As depicted in Fig. 8a, no inhibition zone was formed around the CS-PVA film, indicating CS-PVA cannot kill the bacteria. As for Ag-CP films, 3 % Ag-CP film has the strongest antibacterial activity, whose maximum inhibition zone toward E. coli and S. aureus was 13.8 and 12.8 mm, respectively (Fig. 8b). Since the Gram-positive bacteria B. cereus is a common bacteria that causes food poisoning, we also evaluated the antibacterial effect of 3 % Ag-CP film against B. cereus. As shown in Fig. S4, 3 % Ag-CP film showed significant antibacterial effect on B. cereus, and the average diameter of the inhibition zone was 11.4 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and physicochemical properties of the films

ce:section.ce:para: Different mass ratio (2:1, 1:1 and 1:2) of CS (95 % degree of deacetylation) and PVA solution was mixed to prepare 2CS-PVA, CS-PVA, CS-2PVA films and their mechanical properties were evaluated by tensile strength (TS, Fig. 9a). Among the 2CS-PVA, CS-PVA, CS-2PVA films, CS-PVA film has highest tensile strength of 32.5 MPa. With the increasing of PVA content, intermolecular hydrogen bonds may be formed, thereby enhancing the TS of the film. At the same time, due to the use of chitosan with higher degrees of deacetylation, this will also affect the TS of the film, because chitosan with higher molecular weight (MW) or higher deacetylation may provide more reactive groups within one chain for interaction with PVA through hydrogen bonding, leading to film strengthening [57–59]. However, when the mass ratio of CS to PVA is 1:2, this hydrogen bond balance will be destroyed due to the high content of PVA [60]. Therefore, the matrix of Ag-CP films in the subsequent experiments was CS-PVA. Ag@CDs were doped into CS and PVA to synthesize active packaging films (1 % Ag-CP, 2 % Ag-CP, 3 % Ag-CP, 4 % Ag-CP and 5 % Ag-CP). Because 3 % Ag-CP film has the best antibacterial properties, we also tested the mechanical property of 3 % Ag-CP film, whose tensile strength was enhanced to 39.13 MPa, implying that 3 % Ag@CDs was compatible with the matrix and formed a stable network structure.

Fig. 9b shows the UV–vis transmittance spectra of the films, after the addition of chitosan, the light barrier capacity of the composite film is increased, which may be due to the presence of more hydroxyl and amino auxochrome groups [57]. In addition, the light transmittance gradually decreased with increasing Ag@CDs content, and 3 % Ag-CP film having the highest UV light (200–400 nm) blocking ability, because of the strong UV absorption capacity of Ag@CDs. The low light transmittance of 3 % Ag-CP film could avoid food photooxidation deterioration, thereby extending the storage time of products.

The WCA of PVA, CS, CS-PVA, and 1 % Ag-CP, 2 % Ag-CP and 3 % Ag-CP (Fig. 9c) is 31.5o, 105.1o, 76.6o, 87.8o, 91.4o and 103.4o, respectively. Compared with PVA, the hydrophilicity of CS-PVA was decreased significantly, which may be due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CS and PVA that consuming some exposed hydroxyl groups of PVA [60]. The hydrophilicity of Ag-CP films was decreased with the addition of Ag@CDs, and 3 % Ag-CP has the highest WCA. The interactions between Ag@CDs and CS increase the cohesion and the surface roughness of the Ag-CP film, consequently improving the hydrophobicity of the Ag-CP films [61].

The surface morphologies and cross-section morphologies of CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP films were observed using SEM (Fig. 9d). The surfaces of CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP film were smooth, uniform and free of cracks. No bubbles, cracks and pores were observed in the cross section of the films. Both before and after the addition of Ag@CDs, the CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP films exhibit a continuous and dense morphology, indicating that Ag@CDs were uniformly distributed in the CS-PVA matrix [4]. The EDS elemental mapping verified the uniform distribution of Ag, O, N and C in 3 % Ag-CP film (Fig. S5), indicating that Ag@CDs have been successfully doped into the film. The thickness of CS-PVA film and 3 % Ag-CP film was 20 μm (Fig. S6a, b). It can be seen from Fig. S6c that 3 % Ag-CP film have high transparency. The addition of Ag@CDs NPs did not affect the visual appearance of the packaged product. Moreover, the 3 % Ag-CP film can be expanded without breaking after bending and folding, indicating that the composite film can be used for packaging different shapes of food (Fig. S6d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content (MC), water solubility (WS) and water vapor permeability (WVP) of the films
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the plasticization of water, higher MC results in lower tensile strength [62]. The MC of the composite films gradually decreased with the increase of Ag@CDs content. Thus, the MC of 3 % Ag-CP film was the lowest, which was only 7.06 %. (Fig. 9e). WS reflects the hydrophilicity of films [42], as shown in Fig. 9e, the WS of CS-PVA film was 19.88 %, while the WS of 3 % Ag-CP film was only 10.91 %. This is related to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the reactive groups of CS, PVA and Ag@CDs, which made the internal structure of film more stable and reduced the affinity between the film with water [62]. Water vapor leads to microbial growth, undesirable texture changes, and deteriorative chemical and enzymatic reactions, it is necessary to block the transfer of moisture between the film and air. WVP results showed that 3 % Ag-CP had the lowest WVP (14.86 × 10−11 g.s-1.m-1.Pa−1) (Fig. 9f). Chitosan with high deacetylation has strong interactions with other molecules, and its compact structure restricts the transport of water through the film. At the same time, the addition of Ag@CDs makes the structure of the film more compact and orderly, which makes the water vapor more difficult to go through the pores of the film, and thus reduces the WVP of the film [62].

Pork and shrimp packed with 3 % Ag-CP film were stored at 4 °C for 7 d, and their weight, appearance, bacterial growth and related deterioration indexes were evaluated, respectively. Because of the high fat content, meat products are extremely easy to be oxidized during storage, leading to a series of irreversible changes and the formation of toxic substances, which seriously threaten the health of consumers [10]. As shown in Fig. 10a, pork was divided into three groups: control, CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP. In the control and CS-PVA groups, the myoglobin in the pork was discolored due to oxidation and the surface became darker. In contrast, the pork in 3 % Ag-CP group still maintained good color and luster on day 7. Due to the antibacterial action of 3 % Ag-CP film, photographs of partially enlarged of pork surface showed the lowest number of bacterial colonies on day 7 (Fig. 10b). Based on the National Food Safety Standard of China GB/T 9959.2–2008, the TVC threshold for raw pork is 6.00 log CFU/g [12]. On day 7, the TVC in the control group was 6.54 log CFU/g, which was higher than that in 3 % Ag-CP film group (5.26 log CFU/g) (Fig. 10c). During storage process, because of the lack of film coating, the pork in control group suffered serious weight loss (Fig. 10d), while the weight loss in CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP groups was effectively alleviated. 3 % Ag-CP group had the lowest weight loss, which may be attributed to the fact that 3 % Ag-CP film alleviated water loss and microbial degradation [42]. The pH changes of pork on day 0 and day 7 are shown in Fig. 10e. On day 0, the pH of fresh pork was 6.03. After storage for 7 d, the pork's pH in control, CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP groups was 6.92, 6.60 and 6.40, respectively. Since 3 % Ag-CP film slowed down the proliferation of bacteria, thus retarding the rise of pH [63]. Spoilage of pork is mainly ascribed to the multiplication of bacteria, which further leads to the decomposition of proteins and the production of TVB-N [64]. Based on the Chinese standard GB 2707–2016, the upper limit of TVB-N content in edible pork is 15 mg/100 g [12], As shown in Fig. 10f, with the storage time is prolonged, the content of TVB-N increased gradually. Initial TVB-N content of fresh pork was only 7.79 mg/100 g. On day 7, TVB-N content of pork in control and CS-PVA groups reached to 20.98 mg/100 g and 16.19 mg/100 g, both of them exceeded the upper limit of 15 mg/100 g. The TVB-N content in the 3 % Ag-CP group was only 11.99 mg/100 g, which further reflected that 3 % Ag-CP film had good preservation effect on meat.

The preservation treatment of shrimp is the same as pork. Fig. 11a shows that the color of shrimp gradually deteriorated with the extension of storage time. Photographs of partially enlarged of shrimp showed that shrimp in control and CS-PVA groups had obvious color deepening and serious corruption, while the shrimp in the 3 % Ag-CP group maintained good brightness and luster. Photographs of bacterial colonies on day 7 are presented in Fig. 11b, in the control and CS-PVA groups, a large number of bacteria multiplied on the shrimp. Nevertheless, 3 % Ag-CP film could significantly delay bacterial growth. The TVC in the control, CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP groups was 7.65, 7.32 and 6.37 Log CFU/g, respectively (Fig. 11c). Even after being coated with 3 % Ag-CP film by for 7 days, the number of colonies in the shrimp still did not exceed the national microbiological limit. The weight loss curves of shrimp (Fig. 11d) indicated that 3 % Ag-CP could markedly reduce the weight loss of shrimp. The pH changes are recorded in Fig. 11e, the pH of fresh shrimp was 6.79, 7 days later, the pH in control, CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP groups increased to 7.93, 7.47 and 7.15, respectively. Microorganisms usually induce proteins to decompose into alkaline substances, which leads to pH increase of meat. Even after 7 days of storage, the pH variation of 3 % Ag-CP wrapping shrimp was minimal, demonstrating that 3 % Ag-CP could suppress bacterial proliferation. In paralleled with the TVC and pH changes, the TVB-N in the three groups continuously increased (Fig. 11f). The TVB-N of fresh shrimp was 9.08 mg/100 g, however, the TVB-N in control, CS-PVA and 3 % Ag-CP groups reached 40.69, 30.54 and 22.26 mg/100 g on day 7. The lowest TVB-N in the 3 % Ag-CP group was ascribed to highly effective antibacterial activity of 3 % Ag-CP. The results mentioned above confirmed that 3 % Ag-CP film had long-term antimicrobial performance.

During preservation period, we scored each group of pork and shrimp for color, spoilage, and overall acceptability according to the Sensory Evaluation Grading System, and the average value was taken as final result of each group and plotted. Fig. 12a, b and c are the sensory evaluation results of pork, Fig. 12d, e and f are the sensory evaluation results of shrimp. It can be seen that the scores of colors, spoilage degree and overall acceptability gradually decreased over time. In pork preservation, 3 % Ag-CP group opened a significant gap with the other two groups on day 5. In shrimp preservation, 3 % Ag-CP group already scored significantly higher than the other two groups on day 3. On day 7, pork and shrimp packaged with 3 % Ag-CP film were more acceptable to consumers than the other two groups. The above results fully proved the versatility and practicability of 3 % Ag-CP film in meat preservation. Data tables for sensory evaluations are provided in supporting information (Tables S1–6).

Based on the excellent preservation effect of 3 % Ag-CP film on pork and shrimp at 4 °C, we further evaluated the preservation ability of 3 % Ag-CP film on pork under room temperature. As shown in Fig. 13a and b, After being stored at room temperature for 4 d, the pork in 3 % Ag-CP group were pink and no bacterial colonies appeared on the surface. In contrast, the pork in control group exhibited severe shrinkage and spoilage on day 3, and the pork in CS-PVA group appeared white colonies on day 4. Due to the excellent antibacterial activity of 3 % Ag-CP film, pork in 3 % Ag-CP group had the lowest TVC, weight loss, pH and TVB-N content on day 4 (Fig. 13c-f). The above results proved the universality of 3 % Ag-CP film for food preservation.

The pork in the 3 % Ag-CP group was washed with water (3 mL) and sonicated for 10 min. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the pork washed solution was measured. Compared with 3 % Ag-CP film, no characteristic peak of Ag NPs was observed (Fig. S7a), indicating no Ag NPs residual on the surface of pork after packaging. In addition, due to the solubility of CS in acetic acid solution, the migration of 3 % Ag-CP film in harsh environment was evaluated by using acetic acid aqueous solution (2 %, v/v) as a food simulator [62]. The results showed that after 3 % Ag-CP film was soaked in acetic acid solution for 60 min, the absorption peak of Ag NPs enhanced faintly and the absorbance kept at a low level (below 0.2), indicating the negligible migration level of 3 % Ag-CP film (Fig. S7b).

Ag@CDs were fabricated by simply stirring the mixture of silver nitrate and CDs, which have effective antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. Additionally, Ag@CDs possess potent biofilm destruction capability and biofilm inhibition activity, as well as long-term antimicrobial performance. Ag@CDs were homogeneously incorporated into the matrix of CS and PVA to construct superior active food packaging film (3 % Ag-CP). Ag@CDs could decrease the hydrophilicity, while enhance the mechanical property and UV blocking ability of the films, which was conducive to food preservation. Furthermore, benefiting from the outstanding antibacterial activity, 3 % Ag-CP film was utilized for meat preservation. The results proved that 3 % Ag-CP film could effectively preserved pork and shrimp and prolong their shelf life. On the other hand, further research is needed to assess the preservation effects of the 3 % Ag-CP film on a variety of other foods. Given the evaluated properties, our results confirm the feasibility and convenience of using the 3 % Ag-CP film for food preservation, particularly for high-fat foods, and highlight its promising potential for applications in the active packaging industry.
Yidi Du: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Data curation, Conceptualization.
